scenario_title: Add CNAME where the owner already has other RR types

group_1.behavior: Reject the update because the owner already has incompatible data, leaving the original A and MX RRsets in place.
group_1.post_state: m.campus.edu. still has A and MX; no CNAME is present.

group_2.behavior: Accept the update and resolve the conflict by replacing the existing data with the CNAME.
group_2.post_state: m.campus.edu. has only the CNAME RRset; the A and MX RRsets are removed.

expected_divergence.why_divergence: RFC 2181 §10.1 makes CNAME exclusivity mandatory, but RFC 2136 §3.4.2 does not normatively define whether an UPDATE that adds a CNAME to an owner already holding other RR types must be rejected, implicitly cleaned up, or partially applied. That leaves the server's conflict-resolution policy undefined, producing different reply codes and different resulting zone state.
expected_divergence.test_rationale: The zone starts with m.campus.edu. as a multi-type owner containing A and MX records. A single UPDATE then adds a CNAME at the same owner without prerequisites. Because the protocol text requires CNAME exclusivity but does not specify the UPDATE-time conflict resolution strategy, implementations may diverge either by refusing the update with YXRRSET and preserving the original RRsets or by accepting it and removing the existing data. The follow-up CNAME and A queries make both the reply and the resulting post-state observable.
